metadata.title: The Weekly Harvest: What Engineering Students Should Do With 6,718 Jobs That Refresh Every Seven Days
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.
metadata.author: Nik Bear Brown
metadata.credit_line: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI
tags: The Weekly Harvest: What Engineering Stu
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. As a recent engineering graduate, you assume that an open job is automatically a reachable job. This dataset of thousands of weekly-refreshing listings inverts that assumption. A San Francisco role can be open yet economically inaccessible against $3,100 median monthly rent. With jobs refreshing every seven days, you're really competing on information-processing speed.

artifactLines: As a recent engineering graduate, you assume that an open job is automatically a reachable job.
This dataset of thousands of weekly-refreshing listings inverts that assumption.
A San Francisco role can be open yet economically inaccessible against $3,100 median monthly rent.
With jobs refreshing every seven days, you're really competing on information-processing speed.

beats.narration_text: The inversion that a job can be 'open' yet economically inaccessible, with San Francisco rent at $3,100 against the salary, plus competing on information-processing speed across 6,718 weekly-refreshing jobs, changed how I'd job-hunt. Help me: (1) explain exactly what assumption the dataset inverts, (2) build a filter that screens for economic accessibility rather than mere openness, and (3) design a weekly routine that exploits the seven-day refresh.

This isn’t a static list you bookmark and forget
